role_summary: First full-time executive director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (1960-1964); chief of staff to Martin Luther King Jr.; architect of the 1963 Birmingham Project C campaign; senior pastor of Canaan Baptist Church in Harlem 1967-2004.
ai_reading: On the two axes a reader would expect to anchor a King chief of staff and thirty-seven-year Baptist pastor, the embedding agrees with the cultural record rather than contradicting it: Walker's passages project to +0.87 (normalized) toward the Nonviolence-as-Theology pole and +0.73 toward the Sacred / Theological Framing pole, among the most pole-committed readings in the audited subset of the catalog. On the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis his testimony reads at -0.24, a modest lean toward Strategic Vision. The placement tracks the man cultural memory credits as the grand strategist who designed Project C, yet the lean is mild rather than emphatic, because the same passages also carry the granular register of his own account, walking off distances from the 16th Street headquarters, counting seats in lunch counters, timing how long a march takes, alongside the long-horizon framing the embedding settles on. A second observation: his single strongest semantic neighbor by passage count is C. T. Vivian (entry 134, 28 shared-neighbor hits, cosine peaking at 0.728), and the who-discussed-whom influence graph independently carries an edge from Vivian to Walker with a weight of 3. Cosine clustering and the citation graph rarely converge on the same pair; here both surface the Birmingham co-architect relationship Vivian narrates from his own side. His next neighbor, Timothy Jenkins (entry 120, 22 hits, cosine 0.750), is an NSA-and-SNCC figure rather than an SCLC contemporary, a less obvious pairing than the embedding's third-ranked Lonnie C. King (entry 80) or Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29).
biographical_paragraph: Wyatt Tee Walker grew up a preacher's son in South Jersey, trained as a physics and chemistry major, and turned to the ministry after his experience of segregation in Richmond, Virginia, where he earned his MDiv at Virginia Union University [src: 1]. He pastored Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg for eight years, and on February 27, 1960 he led a sit-in at the segregated Petersburg public library, having requested the first volume of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee, an action that broke the color line at that library [src: 1]. King named him the first full-time executive director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in 1960, after Ella Baker's interim tenure, and Walker served as chief of staff through 1964, building the organization's budget from roughly fifty-seven thousand dollars to a million and its staff to about a hundred [src: 1] [src: 2]. He designed the 1963 Birmingham campaign, which he named Project C for confrontation, scouting downtown targets a year in advance and measuring the distances and seating that the demonstrations would test [src: 1] [src: 3]; he also transcribed and titled King's Letter from Birmingham Jail [src: 1] [src: 4]. From 1967 he led Canaan Baptist Church of Christ in Harlem for thirty-seven years, organized against apartheid in coalition with the African National Congress, and helped win New York's charter-school legislation, founding the Sisulu-Walker Charter School in 1999 [src: 2]. He died on January 23, 2018 [src: 5]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 132 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039); in it he is interviewed alongside his wife Theresa Ann Walker, who recounts her own arrest and beating in 1963 [src: 6]. He discusses Charles Sherrod (whom he says he raised in his Petersburg congregation), the Albany campaign, and his SCLC contemporaries C. T. Vivian (entry 134) and Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29), both of whom came to King's staff from Petersburg.

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how Project C was planned, Walker describes the year of fieldwork he did before the first march:
interview_snippets.quote: I went to Birmingham a year ahead of time.

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: He details the granular survey work that became the campaign blueprint:
interview_snippets.quote: And I went to the stores and I measured how long it would take a young person, a middle-aged person,

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the strategic premise of Project C, that confronting segregation depended on the response of Birmingham's police commissioner Bull Connor:
interview_snippets.quote: And the only way we could join it and we had in the back of our minds that Bull Connor would do something that would help us.

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how important money was to SCLC's campaigns, Walker, who ran the organization's fundraising, answers in three words:

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: Walker recounts naming King's jailhouse essay after the Quakers, who first printed it, had given it a different title:
interview_snippets.quote: I told them, no, it should be called Letter from the Birmingham Jail.

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: C. T. Vivian, who opened the Birmingham campaign at Walker's side, identifies his role at the time:
interview_snippets.quote: Wyatt Walker was Dr. King's chief of staff.

interview_snippets.speaker: Annie Pearl Avery
interview_snippets.lead_in: Birmingham demonstrator Annie Pearl Avery describes being assigned by Walker to a decoy march meant to draw the police away from a larger one:
interview_snippets.quote: Wyatt Tee Walker had me to go on the decoy march,

interview_snippets.speaker: Robert Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Public-relations executive Robert Brown recalls the lunch in Atlanta where he was recruited into King's circle:
interview_snippets.quote: Rev. Wyatt Tee Walker, who was Dr. [Martin Luther] King's right hand man.
